title: Lisas Sax

jokes.text: D'oh. [Screams]
jokes.setup: Homer crashes into something during the opening credits
jokes.punchline: His iconic frustrated grunt and scream
jokes.explanation: Perfect character integration and economy - this is Homer's signature sound, instantly recognizable and quotable

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: [Announcer] The Simpsons is filmed in front of a live studio audience.
jokes.setup: Typical sitcom announcement
jokes.punchline: Applied to an animated show where it makes no sense
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-joke about sitcom conventions being applied to animation - good originality and economy

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: The following program contains scenes of animated violence, language, and adult situations.

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: Viewer discretion is advised.

jokes.text: Hey there, meathead. What are you watchir? Oh, I thought I'd check out the Warner Brothers Network.
jokes.setup: Homer mentions checking out a TV network
jokes.punchline: Dated reference to WB network's struggle for relevance
jokes.explanation: Mild topical humor that feels somewhat dated - relies on cultural reference without much twist

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm gonna check out that new TV network.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Which one, Homer?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The WB.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, the WB went off the air years ago.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, that explains why nobody was there.

jokes.text: with nothing to show for it but four Emmys and a Peabody Award.
jokes.setup: Character lamenting wasted years of drugs and booze
jokes.punchline: The 'nothing to show' is actually prestigious awards
jokes.explanation: Excellent ironic structure - subverts expectation by revealing the 'failure' includes major achievements

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: I spent forty years in show business, drugs, booze, the whole nine yards. And what do I have to show for it?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Nothing! Well, except for three Emmys, a Golden Globe, and a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.

jokes.text: Chan Ho, your mother Mia and I are getting a divorce. Chan Ho is over there. I am Chin Ho. Whoever you are. Just pass it along, kid.
jokes.setup: Krusty tries to tell his son about divorce
jokes.punchline: He's talking to wrong kid, doesn't care, tells him to pass it along anyway
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Krusty's careless parenting, escalates from mistake to indifference

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Hey kid, I gotta tell ya something about life. Sometimes a man and a woman, they just can't make it work together, ya know? So they get a divorce.

dialogue.speaker: Child
dialogue.line: Mr. Krusty, I think you have me confused with your son.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, whatever. Anyway, just pass it along to my boy when you see him, will ya?

jokes.text: [Laughs] What a bad father.
jokes.setup: Witnessed Krusty's terrible parenting
jokes.punchline: Bart's matter-of-fact assessment
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective deadpan reaction - Bart's casual observation adds to the absurdity

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Man, and I thought my dad was a bad parent.

jokes.text: You'll practice me? What does that mean? Is it supposed to be some sort of threat?
jokes.setup: Homer says 'I'll practice you!' in anger
jokes.punchline: Lisa logically deconstructs his nonsensical threat
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa character moment - her logical mind questioning adult illogic with genuine confusion

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'll practice you!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, that doesn't make any sense. You can't practice a person. Practice is the repetition of an activity to improve skill. I am not an activity.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah? My dear Lisa, you are eight, and I am 10. And in my two extra years on this planet... I've learned a few tricks.
jokes.setup: Lisa thinks she can resist Bart taking her saxophone
jokes.punchline: Bart's grandiose speech about his vast experience of being 2 years older
jokes.explanation: Excellent character writing - Bart's pompous delivery about minimal age difference is perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Lisa, you fool! I have 2 years of experience on you. I've seen things you couldn't possibly imagine. I was there when they invented Pop-Tarts. I watched the Berlin Wall fall. I've been to Krusty Burger at least... *counts on fingers* ...hundreds of times.

jokes.text: Ha-ha!
jokes.setup: Lisa's saxophone is destroyed
jokes.punchline: Nelson's signature mocking laugh
jokes.explanation: Perfect character integration and economy - Nelson's laugh is instantly recognizable and perfectly timed

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Ha-ha!

jokes.text: if it'll make you feel better, I'll destroy something Bart loves. Hey! Don't worry, Son. If that bothers you, I'll destroy something Maggie loves.
jokes.setup: Homer tries to comfort Lisa about her broken sax
jokes.punchline: His solution escalates to destroying everyone's belongings in sequence
jokes.explanation: Great escalation structure and perfect Homer logic - his 'solution' just creates more problems

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: There, there, sweetie. Your sax is broken? Don't worry, I'll fix it.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Really, Dad?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Sure! I'll just take it apart and— oh no, I broke it worse.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No problem! I'll buy you a new one. Marge, where's my wallet?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, no!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Don't worry, I'll just destroy your things to make room for the new sax. Goodbye, TV!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, what are you doing?!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Making space! Goodbye, couch! Goodbye, refrigerator! Goodbye, house!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, stop!

jokes.text: Back then, The Artist Formerly Known as Prince was currently known as Prince.
jokes.setup: Homer sets up the time period
jokes.punchline: Clever wordplay about Prince's name changes
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay that perfectly captures the absurdity of Prince's name evolution - very quotable

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Back in 1993, Prince changed his name to that unpronounceable symbol.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I tried to call him, but the phone kept disconnecting. I guess you could say we had symbol problems.

jokes.text: Tracey Ullman was entertaining America... with songs, sketches and crudely drawn filler material.
jokes.setup: Setting up the time period with Tracey Ullman show
jokes.punchline: The Simpsons referring to themselves as 'crudely drawn filler material'
jokes.explanation: Exceptional meta-humor acknowledging the show's humble origins - self-aware and historically accurate

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We're just crudely drawn filler material.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Yeah, they'll never make a series out of us.

jokes.text: Homer, you're dumb as a mule and twice as ugly. If a strange man offers you a ride, I say take it.
jokes.setup: Homer recalls his father's advice for first day of school
jokes.punchline: Terrible parenting advice that's both insulting and dangerous
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grandpa character moment - combines insult with dangerously bad advice in classic fashion

dialogue.speaker: Grandpa Simpson
dialogue.line: Son, if the kids pick on you, just pick on them back, but meaner. And if a teacher asks you a question you don't know, just say 'I dunno' real loud so everyone thinks you're retarded.

jokes.text: Lousy traumatic childhood.
jokes.setup: Just recalled his father's terrible advice
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual dismissal of obvious trauma
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy and character consistency - Homer's casual tone about serious trauma is perfectly him

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dad's advice was terrible. He told me, 'Son, if something's hard to do, then it's not worth doing.' So I just stick with what's easy and stupid.

jokes.text: I'm Principal Sinner- Skinner. [Laughing] Well, that's it. I've lost them forever.
jokes.setup: Skinner introduces himself to kindergarteners
jokes.punchline: Mispronounces his own name as 'Sinner', kids laugh, he immediately gives up
jokes.explanation: Good wordplay combined with perfect Skinner defeatism - his immediate surrender is very in character

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Hello children, I'm Principal Skinner.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: I mean, Principal Sinner.

dialogue.speaker: Kids
dialogue.line: Ha ha!

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Oh, forget it.

jokes.text: Well, that's it. I've lost them forever.
jokes.setup: Children laughing at his mispronunciation
jokes.punchline: Skinner's immediate defeatist reaction to minor mistake
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Skinner's neurotic insecurity

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Oh, for the love of... Well, that's it. I'm finished. My career is over. I might as well just walk into the sea right now.

jokes.text: and Jimbo, the school bully... who will administer noogies and nipple twisters. I look forward to whaling on all of you.
jokes.setup: Skinner introducing school staff
jokes.punchline: Officially introduces the bully as if it's a normal school position
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of school introduction tropes - treating bullying as official school service

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: And this is Jimbo Jones, our Director of Bullying.

dialogue.speaker: Jimbo
dialogue.line: Thanks, Principal Skinner.

jokes.text: Added extra clap. Not college material.
jokes.setup: Children singing and clapping
jokes.punchline: Teacher writes off kid's future for minor musical mistake
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy and dark humor - the extreme overreaction to tiny mistake is perfectly absurd

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: That's it. Your future is ruined.

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: But I only played one wrong note!

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: One wrong note is all it takes.

jokes.text: [Shouts In Thick Scottish Accent] Can't you understand English?
jokes.setup: Willie yells at Bart in thick Scottish accent
jokes.punchline: Asks if Bart can understand English while being nearly incomprehensible himself
jokes.explanation: Perfect Willie moment - the irony of his incomprehensible accent while demanding English comprehension

dialogue.speaker: Groundskeeper Willie
dialogue.line: Can ye understand English, ye wee bampot?! I'm speakin' plain as day in the Queen's own language!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Uh, sorry Willie, I have no idea what you just said.

jokes.text: D'oh!
jokes.setup: Lisa points out he told the wrong story
jokes.punchline: Homer's signature frustrated grunt
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer reaction - perfect timing and character integration for his signature catchphrase

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Come on, Maggie, it's Miller time. Yes, it's Miller time.
jokes.setup: Homer offers to help with baby Maggie
jokes.punchline: His solution is to give beer to an infant
jokes.explanation: Dark humor typical of Homer's parenting approach - cultural reference adds to the absurdity

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Don't worry, I know how to handle babies. I've got this.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Here you go, sweetie. A nice cold Duff will put you right to sleep.

jokes.text: Oh, my father gave me beer as a child... till I wrapped my little red wagon around a tree.
jokes.setup: Homer justifies giving beer to babies
jokes.punchline: Reveals his childhood drinking led to drunk driving a wagon into a tree
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark escalation - child-sized drunk driving accident is perfectly absurd backstory

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, a little beer never hurt anybody. I gave Bart some when he was a baby and look how he turned out!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: My dad gave me beer when I was a kid. One time I got so drunk, I drove the wagon right into a tree.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But I turned out fine!

jokes.text: Let's never drink again. And we never did.
jokes.setup: Family swears off drinking after Homer's story
jokes.punchline: Homer claims they never drank again (obviously false)
jokes.explanation: Good ironic humor - Homer's obviously false claim given his well-established drinking habits

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And that's the story of how our family swore off drinking forever.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: We never touched a drop again.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Not once.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: It changed our lives.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yep, best decision we ever made. Now let's celebrate with a beer!

jokes.text: Hey, she's just giving the public what it wants- Bart by the barrelful.
jokes.setup: Lisa complains everything becomes about Bart
jokes.punchline: Bart acknowledges his popularity with theatrical flair
jokes.explanation: Good meta-joke about Bart's popularity as a character while staying in character as an egotistical kid

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Everything always becomes about you, Bart!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Ay caramba, what can I say? I'm a star.

jokes.text: "F," Bart. And believe me, you'll be seeing plenty of'em.
jokes.setup: Bart struggles with alphabet, asks for help
jokes.punchline: Teacher's prediction about his academic future
jokes.explanation: Solid setup/punchline structure with dark humor about educational failure - good economy

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: Bart, you're having trouble with the alphabet. At this rate, you'll never graduate.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: So what? I'll just become a rock star or something.

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: Yes, well, you can't even spell 'rock star.'

jokes.text: Would you like to color something? I did.
jokes.setup: Teacher asks if Bart wants to color
jokes.punchline: Reveals he already did (presumably something inappropriate)
jokes.explanation: Implies visual gag we can't see - typical Bart misbehavior with good economy

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: Bart, would you like to color?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I already did.

jokes.text: Brilliant. [Laughs] I have absolutely no idea what's going on.
jokes.setup: Homer watches Twin Peaks on TV
jokes.punchline: Calls it brilliant while admitting complete confusion
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - his tendency to praise things he doesn't understand, good cultural reference

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: This is brilliant! I have no idea what's going on.

jokes.text: [Screams] Burn it! Send it to hell!
jokes.setup: Marge asks Homer to look at Bart's drawing
jokes.punchline: Homer's extreme terrified reaction to whatever Bart drew
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from casual to extreme terror - implies visual gag we can't see but Homer's reaction sells it

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, look at Bart's drawing.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: AHHHHHHH! Why would he draw that?! This is the most horrifying thing I've ever seen! I'm gonna have nightmares for weeks!

jokes.text: When a boy doesn't want to play catch with his old man, something is seriously wrong. I'll play catch with you, Son. Get the hell out. I'm gone.
jokes.setup: Homer laments Bart won't play catch, Grandpa offers
jokes.punchline: Homer's immediate harsh rejection and Grandpa's quick retreat
jokes.explanation: Good character interaction showing Homer's hypocrisy and family dysfunction with rapid escalation

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No way, Dad. You throw like a girl.

dialogue.speaker: Grandpa
dialogue.line: I am a girl.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Exactly.

dialogue.speaker: Grandpa
dialogue.line: Well, I'm going home.

jokes.text: particularly one with as many flamboyantly homosexual tendencies as your son.
jokes.setup: Psychologist explains why public school is intimidating
jokes.punchline: Completely inappropriate and wrong assessment
jokes.explanation: Dated humor that feels inappropriate by current standards - relies on harmful stereotypes

dialogue.speaker: School Psychologist
dialogue.line: You see, public school can be very intimidating for a young child. There's the loud noise, the chaos, the aggressive students...

dialogue.speaker: School Psychologist
dialogue.line: But the real problem is that your son has an unhealthy relationship with his mother.

jokes.text: Bart's gay? Bart? Uh-Whoo! Wrong file.
jokes.setup: Homer's shock at the assessment
jokes.punchline: Psychologist reveals he was reading wrong file
jokes.explanation: Misdirection joke that partially redeems the previous inappropriate comment but still problematic

dialogue.speaker: School Psychologist
dialogue.line: Mr. Simpson, I've reviewed Bart's psychological profile. He's actually quite intelligent.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What?! That can't be right!

dialogue.speaker: School Psychologist
dialogue.line: Oh wait, I was reading your file.

jokes.text: that Bart must learn to be less of an individual... and more a faceless slug.
jokes.setup: Psychologist gives advice about school
jokes.punchline: Brutally honest assessment of public education goals
jokes.explanation: Sharp satirical observation about education system with excellent economy and dark honesty

dialogue.speaker: School Psychologist
dialogue.line: The goal of public education isn't to fill young minds with knowledge. It's to socialize children into the middle class.

jokes.text: Marge, he's five. His life is over. Lisa's the wave of the future.
jokes.setup: Marge asks if anything can be done for Bart
jokes.punchline: Psychologist's brutally matter-of-fact dismissal of a 5-year-old
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor with perfect economy - the casual dismissal of a child's entire future is darkly hilarious

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Is there anything that can be done for Bart?

dialogue.speaker: School Psychologist
dialogue.line: Well, I'm afraid Bart is what we call a problem child. He's lazy, inattentive, and has no respect for authority. In short, he's the perfect student.

dialogue.speaker: School Psychologist
dialogue.line: No, I'm just kidding. He's a bad seed.

jokes.text: [Man] Lisa Simpson has won the Nobel Rrize for kickboxing.
jokes.setup: Homer imagines Lisa's future success
jokes.punchline: Absurd Nobel Prize category that doesn't exist
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor combining prestigious award with inappropriate category

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: And the Nobel Prize for Procrastination goes to... Lisa Simpson!

jokes.text: Over 75% of our graduates go on to first grade.
jokes.setup: Administrator boasts about preschool quality
jokes.punchline: The 'achievement' is just normal progression
jokes.explanation: Good satire of educational marketing - treating normal progression as major achievement

dialogue.speaker: Preschool Administrator
dialogue.line: Our preschool has an outstanding achievement record. Why, just last week, one of our students learned to use the toilet!

dialogue.speaker: Preschool Administrator
dialogue.line: And another one figured out how to tie his shoes. We're very proud of these accomplishments.

jokes.text: I'll give you 50 bucks. Seventy-five.
jokes.setup: Told tuition is $6,000
jokes.punchline: Homer's ridiculously low counter-offers
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer cheapskate moment - the huge gap between price and offer is perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Six thousand dollars? I'll give you twenty bucks.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, wait... I'll go up to thirty.

jokes.text: Excelente. Muchos gracias, "señorata." Sorry. [Chinese Accent] Aah, so.
jokes.setup: Looking for minority scholarships
jokes.punchline: Homer's terrible attempts at fake accents and ethnicities
jokes.explanation: Dated humor relying on ethnic stereotypes - feels cheap and problematic by current standards

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Looking for minority scholarships

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm a troubled youth from the inner city.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Howdy, I'm an inner city single mother.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Me love you long time. I come from the Orient.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oy vey, I'm a Jewish man with a funny accent.

jokes.text: And sometimes I feel so smothered by this family... I just wanna scream till my lungs explode. [Gasps] I'll go start dinner now.
jokes.setup: Family discussion about past problems
jokes.punchline: Marge's sudden emotional breakdown followed by immediate return to domestic role
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment showing Marge's repressed frustration and immediate return to expected role

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I've spent years cleaning up after all of you, sacrificing my own dreams, my own life!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Sometimes I wonder if anyone even appreciates what I do around here!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Of course we do, Marge.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Do you? Do you really?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I just... I can't... I'm sorry, I don't know what came over me.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Now, who wants meatloaf for dinner?

jokes.text: The year is 19-aught-six. The president is the divine Miss Sarah Bernardt. And all over America... people were doir a dance called the Funky Grandpa.
jokes.setup: Grandpa offers to tell the story
jokes.punchline: Completely wrong time period with absurd historical details
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grandpa character moment - his confused sense of history and the 'Funky Grandpa' dance

dialogue.speaker: Grandpa Simpson
dialogue.line: I'll tell ya, back in my day, we didn't have none of these fancy smartphones. We had to use two tin cans connected by string, and half the time the string was eaten by raccoons.

dialogue.speaker: Grandpa Simpson
dialogue.line: And President Teddy Roosevelt, he'd come over to our house for dinner every Sunday. He'd bring his pet dinosaur and we'd roast it over the fire pit.

dialogue.speaker: Grandpa Simpson
dialogue.line: Life was simpler then. Sure, we had to walk uphill both ways to school, but at least we didn't have to worry about aliens invading like they did in 1887.

jokes.text: set way back four billion years ago... when the Earth was just a ball of molten lava.
jokes.setup: Weather report about heat records
jokes.punchline: Compares current heat to Earth's formation
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist weather reporting - the extreme historical comparison for heat record

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: It's so hot, it's like the Earth is being formed all over again.

jokes.text: Unless- Museums don't have foosball, do they?
jokes.setup: Homer initially rejects museum idea
jokes.punchline: His sudden interest based on completely wrong assumption
jokes.explanation: Good Homer character moment - his complete misunderstanding of what museums offer

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, we should take the children to the museum this weekend.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The museum? Marge, that's for nerds and people who read.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But Dad, they have an exhibit on ancient civilizations and—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No way. I'm not wasting a Saturday looking at old rocks.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: They have a gift shop, Dad.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A gift shop? With merchandise? Why didn't you say so? I'll drive!

jokes.text: You lose, Michelangelo's David. Who's next? Me!
jokes.setup: Homer imagines museum with foosball
jokes.punchline: Playing foosball against famous statue
jokes.explanation: Creative visual gag combining high art with lowbrow entertainment - good originality

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm playing foosball against the statue of David!

dialogue.speaker: Statue of David
dialogue.line: You're going down, Simpson!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Bring it on, marble boy!

jokes.text: Balzac. No need for potty mouth just because you can't think of one.
jokes.setup: Marge names successful person without air conditioning
jokes.punchline: Homer thinks 'Balzac' is profanity
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay showing Homer's ignorance while Marge demonstrates education - great character contrast

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Balzac is a very successful person without air conditioning.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Balzac?! Marge, you can't say that!

jokes.text: Well, sir, I hate to be a suspicious Aloysius on you... but did you steal my air conditioner?
jokes.setup: Ned confronts Homer about theft
jokes.punchline: His attempt at rhyming slang with 'suspicious Aloysius'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Flanders character moment - his awkward attempt at casual slang while being polite about theft

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Homer, I noticed my lawnmower is missing. Did you take it?

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Suspicious Aloysius? I don't know what you're talking about!

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: That's not even rhyming slang, Homer.

jokes.text: Well, I admit it looks bad, Flanders, but haven't you heard of... "Let he who is without sin cast the first stone"?
jokes.setup: Caught red-handed with stolen air conditioner
jokes.punchline: Homer misuses biblical quote to avoid accountability
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's tendency to misappropriate authority to escape consequences

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Let he who is without sin cast the first stone.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's not what that means.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, nobody's throwing stones, so I'm in the clear!

jokes.text: So you see, children, there is hope for anyone. Even me? No.
jokes.setup: Teacher tells uplifting story about ugly duckling
jokes.punchline: Immediately crushes individual student's hopes
jokes.explanation: Excellent setup/punchline with perfect economy - dark subversion of uplifting message

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: You see, class, the ugly duckling was mocked and rejected, but he never gave up. He persevered and became a beautiful swan.

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: The moral is: you can achieve anything if you believe in yourself.

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: Now, Ralph, in your case... well, that doesn't really apply.

jokes.text: I wish I was interesting like you. [Imitates Fart] [Laughing] You're funny. I am? Yeah.
jokes.setup: Student wishes he was interesting like Bart
jokes.punchline: Bart's simple fart noise makes him a comedy genius to the kid
jokes.explanation: Shows Bart discovering his comedic power but relies on basic potty humor

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: I wish I was interesting like you, Bart.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: *makes fart noise*

dialogue.speaker: Student
dialogue.line: You're a comedy genius!

jokes.text: Young man, I can assure you... my posterior is nothing more than flesh, bone and that metal plate I got in 'Nam.
jokes.setup: Teacher responds to Bart's fart noises
jokes.punchline: Overly detailed explanation including Vietnam War injury
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from simple reprimand to unexpected personal history - the specificity makes it funny

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Pppppppphhhhh

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: That's quite enough, Mr. Simpson. You know, that reminds me of the time I was in Vietnam. A land mine went off near me, and I lost all control of my intestinal tract. For three weeks, I made sounds just like that. My sergeant said, 'Soldier, you're a one-man noise machine.' It was the lowest point of my life.

jokes.text: Eat my shorts.
jokes.setup: Principal asks for response to lecture
jokes.punchline: Bart's signature catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Iconic Bart catchphrase with perfect character integration and economy - culturally significant

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Eat my shorts!

jokes.text: You have 13 pickled eggs in this jar... and one cockroach.
jokes.setup: Moe asking who Lisa is at the bar
jokes.punchline: Three-year-old Lisa casually pointing out health violations
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Lisa's observational skills even as toddler

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Who's this?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I'm Lisa Simpson. And I couldn't help but notice your establishment is in direct violation of the health code on at least seventeen counts.

jokes.text: Who are you, sweetheart, the health inspector? No, but I am. Uh, here, have a margarita. Uh, that's a parasol.
jokes.setup: Lisa points out problems at Moe's bar
jokes.punchline: Real health inspector appears, Moe offers drink that's just umbrella
jokes.explanation: Good escalation and visual gag - the parasol instead of drink shows bar's terrible state

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Your bar has serious health code violations.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Health code? I don't know nothin' about no health code.

dialogue.speaker: Health Inspector
dialogue.line: I'm the real health inspector, and this place is a disaster.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Can I get you a drink? How about a nice mai tai?

dialogue.speaker: Health Inspector
dialogue.line: Sure.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Here ya go.

dialogue.speaker: Health Inspector
dialogue.line: This is just an umbrella.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, it's an umbrella drink now.

jokes.text: "Viomalin"? "Tubamaba"? "Oboemoboe"? That one. Oh, "saxomaphone."
jokes.setup: Homer tries to say instrument names
jokes.punchline: His mangled pronunciations of musical instruments
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's lack of sophistication, 'saxomaphone' becomes memorable

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A cello? That's a big violin.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: An oboe? Sounds like a skin condition.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A harpsichord? That's just a fancy piano that got electrocuted.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, those are completely wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, what do you call that thing with all the strings? A... guitarmaphone?

jokes.text: To Lisa: Never forget your daddy loves- D'oh!
jokes.setup: Homer tries to write touching inscription
jokes.punchline: Makes mistake, ends inscription with his frustrated grunt
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Homer's good intentions ruined by his signature incompetence

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: To my dearest family, I love you more than donuts, beer, and TV combined. You mean everything to... D'oh!

jokes.text: What origin? We get 'em for $ 1.95 down at the Safeway.
jokes.setup: Homer offers to tell origin story of Maggie's pacifier
jokes.punchline: Deflates expectation with mundane reality
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of dramatic storytelling with prosaic reality - classic Homer practicality

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You wanna know where Maggie's pacifier came from? It's quite a story. Well, actually, no, it's not. I bought it at the store.

jokes.text: Yeah, not to mention lookir like a pig, eatir like a pig- Don't forget the smell.
jokes.setup: Homer complains about sweating like a pig
jokes.punchline: Kids pile on with additional pig comparisons
jokes.explanation: Simple escalation joke - kids adding insults but somewhat predictable

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm sweating like a pig!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, and you eat like one too.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Plus you're about as smart as a pig.

dialogue.speaker: Maggie
dialogue.line: *sucks pacifier*

jokes.text: "Dear Lisa... may your new saxophone bring you years of d'oh!"
jokes.setup: Lisa reads the inscription on her saxophone
jokes.punchline: Homer's mistake is permanently memorialized
jokes.explanation: Nice callback that ties the story together - Homer's incompetence becomes permanent fixture

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: "To Lisa, never give up your music. Love, Dad."

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Wait, Dad can't write. Homer must have had someone else inscribe this.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: "To Lisa, never give up your music. Love, Dad." Oh no... it says "Love, Dad" but Dad's name is Homer!

jokes.text: Where's Maggie? I'm not kiddir. I can't see. My retinas have detached again.
jokes.setup: Family realizes Maggie is missing
jokes.punchline: Grandpa's casual mention of serious medical condition
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Grandpa - treats serious medical issue as minor inconvenience

dialogue.speaker: Grandpa Simpson
dialogue.line: Well, I haven't seen her since this morning when I was teaching her to play with my revolver.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dad!

dialogue.speaker: Grandpa Simpson
dialogue.line: What? It's not loaded. Well, not anymore. Lost the bullets in the war. And some other stuff.

jokes.text: [Homer] Lisa! Enough "saxomaphone" already!
jokes.setup: Lisa playing saxophone beautifully
jokes.punchline: Homer still mispronounces it and wants her to stop
jokes.explanation: Good callback that maintains character consistency - Homer's still the same despite the sweet story

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa, your saxaphone playing is really beautiful, but it's giving me a headache. Can you stop?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, it's pronounced 'saxophone.'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's what I said, saxaphone. Now please stop before my brain explodes.

tmdb_overview: After Lisa’s saxophone is destroyed, the Simpsons reminisce about how she originally got the instrument. On the hottest day of the year, Homer must decide whether to buy an air conditioner or replace Lisa’s sax.
insight: Flashback structure carries 72-point episode despite modest joke density of 3.14 per minute.
